nativeRange.habitat: Type locality is Hoa Creek near Teminabuan village on the Bird's Head (Vogelkop / Kepala Burung) Peninsula of West Papua, Indonesia. The creek is generally described as clear, fast-flowing in places, with a sandy and rocky bottom. Closely related congeners in the umbrella (Cherax pulverulentus, Cherax warsamsonicus) occur in nearby drainages: C. pulverulentus from streams west of Ayamaru Lake in the Framu Subdistrict of Southwest Papua, and C. warsamsonicus from the Warsamson River drainage in the western Vogelkop.

decorPreferences: sand or smooth gravel substrate to allow light digging
rocky caves, overhangs and tight crevices
PVC pipe sections, bricks or terracotta pots as narrow hides
brown leaf litter (oak, beech, almond) for grazing and shelter
dim lighting with shaded retreats — generally nocturnal
tightly fitting weighted tank lid (escape-proof — will climb air hoses and silicone)
robust filtration with strong aeration and gentle-to-moderate flow

diet.notes: Generally regarded as an opportunistic omnivore with a vegetable bias as adults. Has been documented eating sinking crayfish pellets and wafers, blanched vegetables (spinach, pumpkin, peas, zucchini, nettle), brown autumn leaves, frozen bloodworm, mysis shrimp and prawn pieces. Protein roughly 20-30% of the overall diet is commonly recommended, with higher protein for juveniles. Periodic calcium/mineral supplementation is widely cited as supporting successful molts. Will readily predate small live prey such as snails and dwarf shrimp.

goodWith: species-only single-specimen setups in heavily decorated tanks
1M:1-2F harem configurations in large tanks with abundant hiding spots and even sex distribution
fast mid- and upper-level fish well above claw reach in oversized tanks (with caveats)
avoidWith: slow-moving or long-finned fish — risk of claw injuries
bottom-dwelling fish (corydoras, loaches, plecos) sharing the substrate
other crayfish, especially conspecific males (territorial conflicts, post-molt cannibalism)
congeneric Cherax species — known to hybridize, contaminating species lines
North American crayfish (Procambarus, etc.) — crayfish-plague transmission risk
live aquarium plants — will cut, shred and uproot most rooted plants

imageCandidates.notes: Christian Lukhaup; 1268x810. Striking live in-water specimen on gravel showing all diagnostic features: electric-blue chelae with cream/white tips, magenta-pink cephalothorax, pink-banded dark blue/purple pleon, blue legs. Sharp, whole animal.

Christian Lukhaup; 1268x736. Second live in-water individual, different colour phase (greenish body, pink cephalothorax, blue claws with cream tips); sharp, whole animal, shows the complex's colour range.
Christian Lukhaup; 1268x645. Third live individual on sand, olive/blue natural phase with blue chelae; sharp, whole animal, adds variety to the umbrella colour range.

summary: The Blue Moon Crayfish (Cherax pulcher) is a vividly colored freshwater crayfish from Hoa Creek on the Vogelkop Peninsula of West Papua, Indonesia, growing to around 8–15 cm. Together with two closely related Papuan species, it has been exported under trade names including Hoa Creek, Blue Moon, Irian Jaya, and Thunderbolt.
careNotes:
UMBRELLA ENTRY covering the West Papua Cherax trade complex. The trade names 'Hoa Creek', 'Blue Moon', 'Irian Jaya', and 'Pink Coral' are applied not only to Cherax pulcher — formally described by Lukhaup in 2015 from Hoa Creek near Teminabuan on the Vogelkop Peninsula — but also to two closely related species exported from the same general region: Cherax pulverulentus, formally described in 2025 from streams west of Ayamaru Lake, which had been commercially confused with C. pulcher for roughly 21 years before receiving a formal name; and Cherax warsamsonicus, described in 2017 from the Warsamson River drainage in the western Vogelkop. All three species are genetically and morphologically similar, share overlapping color phases (pinks, greens, blues, and white or cream chela edges), and are typically not distinguished at point of sale. Husbandry guidance in this entry applies to the umbrella complex as a whole.

Wild C. pulcher coloration — as documented in the original species description — features a pink cephalothorax (the fused head-and-body section common to all crayfish) fading into greenish-grey on the sides, a greenish-blue rostrum (the forward-pointing projection above the head), a dark blue to black pleon (the segmented abdomen) edged in pinkish-grey, blue legs, and blue chelae (claws) with cream or white tips. Selectively bred and locally collected morphs extend the color range to predominantly blue, white-blue-violet, and other forms.

Like all decapods, the Blue Moon Crayfish is generally considered copper-sensitive — copper-based medications and snail treatments containing copper should be avoided in any tank housing these animals.

The species is widely reported as a skilled escape artist; a tightly fitting, weighted lid is broadly regarded as essential, as individuals have been documented climbing air hoses and silicone seams to exit the aquarium.

NOT plant-safe: the Blue Moon Crayfish is generally reported to cut, shred, and uproot most rooted aquarium plants, making it incompatible with planted-tank designs. Robust epiphytes attached to hardscape, or artificial plants, can be used if greenery is desired.

Temperament: some sources characterize the species as aggressive, reporting fish predation and tankmate fighting; others describe it as peaceful overall with occasional opportunistic hunting. The aggressive characterization is more broadly supported by consistent reports of dwarf-shrimp predation and the animal's adult size, though individual behavior varies.

LEGAL STATUS — READ BEFORE PURCHASE: import or possession of all Cherax species is prohibited in California, Florida, Texas, and Wisconsin.
breedingNotes: The Blue Moon Crayfish is generally regarded as relatively easy to breed at warm tropical temperatures, though markedly less prolific than North American crayfish species. Sexual maturity has been reported to occur at around 8 to 10 months of age, when individuals reach approximately 5 to 6 cm in body length. Females typically carry roughly 40 to 80 eggs under the abdomen for about 4 to 6 weeks before hatching, with larger individuals occasionally producing clutches of up to around 200 eggs. Like other members of the genus Cherax, there is no free-swimming larval stage — juveniles emerge as fully formed miniatures of the adults. Keeping one male with one or two females in a separate breeding tank, providing abundant food, dense narrow hiding spots, and promptly separating juveniles to limit cannibalism, are all widely recommended. Hybridization with related Cherax species — including the closely related C. pulverulentus, C. warsamsonicus, and C. boesemani — has been reported in mixed-species tanks and should be avoided if maintaining a pure line is desired.

primary.name: Aqua Imports (Papuan Crayfish - Cherax pulcher)
primary.notes: Designated primary The Shrimp Farm returned HTTP 403 with no accessible Cherax pulcher species page; substituted Aqua Imports product page as primary. Source for adult size 4-5 inches (10-12.7 cm), 20 US gal (76 L), 75-80°F (24-27°C), pH 7.0-7.8, wild-caught Indonesia, aggressive temperament with documented fish predation, tight-fitting lid required due to climbing, opportunistic scavenger diet, hiding-place preference, and the US state-level import bans (California, Florida, Texas, Wisconsin).

additional.name: AquariumBreeder (Cherax pulcher Detailed Guide)
additional.notes: Secondary cross-source. Confirmed adult size 10-12 cm (4-5 in), lifespan 2-5 years, temperature 22-26°C, pH 6.5-7.5, gH 4-12, kH 1-6, 15-20 US gal (60-80 L) per individual, aggressive/territorial temperament, omnivore 20-30% protein diet, escape-artist behavior (climbs air hoses and silicone), copper sensitivity, NOT plant-safe (cuts/shreds/uproots), breeding ~50 eggs with 30-40 day incubation and sexual maturity at 8-10 months. Lists trade names: Hoa Creek, Blue Moon, Cherax Irian Jaya, Colorful Crayfish, Thunderbolt Crayfish, Pink Coral Crayfish, Rosemoon Crayfish.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Cherax pulcher) / Lukhaup 2015 description
additional.notes: Taxonomic and type-locality anchor. Family Parastacidae, order Decapoda, subgenus Astaconephrops, described 2015 by Lukhaup from Hoa Creek near Teminabuan, Vogelkop Peninsula, West Papua, Indonesia. Type-series total length males 75-98 mm, females 83-90 mm. Distinguishing coloration: pink cephalothorax, greenish-blue rostrum, dark blue/black pleon with pinkish-grey margins, blue legs, blue chelae with cream/white edges. Lists 'Hoa Creek', 'Blue Moon' and 'Irian Jaya' as marketed trade names. Closest morphological relative is C. boesemani (collected ~22 km away).

additional.name: Rendo Shrimp (Hoa Creek Crayfish - Cherax pulcher)
additional.notes: Third cross-source. Adult size 10-15 cm, lifespan up to 5 years, temperature 20-25°C (prefers cooler end), pH 6.5-7.5, gH up to 20, kH up to 10, minimum 80 cm tank per pair, characterized as 'peaceful overall' (disagrees with US sources) with occasional hunting behavior, omnivore diet including leaves and frozen foods, 40-80 eggs hatching after 4-6 weeks, juveniles need separate tank. Confirms NOT plant-safe.

additional.name: Zootaxa / Lukhaup et al. 2025 (Cherax pulverulentus description) — umbrella consolidation rationale
additional.notes: Used to document the umbrella consolidation. Cherax pulverulentus, described in 2025 by Lukhaup and colleagues from streams west of Ayamaru Lake (Framu Subdistrict, Southwest Papua Province), had been exported under 'Hoa Creek', 'Irian Jaya' and 'Blue Moon' trade names for ~21 years before formal naming and is genetically and morphologically most similar to C. pulcher. Cherax warsamsonicus (described 2017, Warsamson River, western Vogelkop) is also sold as 'Hoa Creek', 'Pink Coral' and 'Irian Jaya' and is similarly confused with C. pulcher at point of sale. This justifies the umbrella treatment per the user's hybrid-consolidation directive.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Generally molts frequently as a juvenile (commonly reported every few weeks during active growth) and progressively less often as a mature adult. The shed exoskeleton should be left in the tank — it is typically re-consumed for calcium recycling. Stable parameters and adequate gH/calcium are widely cited as the most important factors in successful molts. Has been reported to seek a secluded narrow hide before molting and to remain vulnerable for several days afterward; tankmates that harass post-molt individuals are a frequent cause of loss, so dense narrow cover is essential.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Generally considered easy to breed at warm tropical temperatures. Females carry roughly 40 to 80 eggs (up to around 200 in larger individuals) under the abdomen for 4 to 6 weeks before hatching; there is no free-swimming larval stage and juveniles emerge as miniature adults. Sexual maturity is generally reported at around 8 to 10 months of age and approximately 5 to 6 cm body length. Hybridization with related Cherax species — including C. pulverulentus and C. warsamsonicus within this complex, as well as C. boesemani — has been reported and should be avoided to maintain pure lines. See the top-level breedingNotes for full discussion.
